thread.title: Fife and Gallo reelected to Oakland City Council. Bas wins supervisor race
thread.title_full: Fife and Gallo reelected to Oakland City Council. Bas wins supervisor race

author: Natalie Orenstein
title: Fife and Gallo reelected to Oakland City Council. Bas wins supervisor race
text:
After a tumultuous few weeks since the Nov. 5 election, the winners and losers in the Oakland and Alameda County races are all but certain. The Registrar of Voters has said the final results — including another small update from 4,800 ballots that have signature issues and need to be cured — will be released Dec. 3. In most races, candidates have issued victory or concession announcements. Other media have also called many of these races.
2024 election
Take a closer look at the results .
The two incumbents running for Oakland City Council — District 3’s Carroll Fife and District 5’s Noel Gallo — handily held onto their seats. Three newcomers, Rowena Brown (at-large), Zac Unger (District 1), and Ken Houston (District 7) will be sworn in in January. Ryan Richardson will become the new city attorney next year.
Special elections will be held in the spring for mayor, following the overwhelming recall of Sheng Thao , and for the District 2 council seat, which Nikki Fortunato Bas is vacating for a county supervisor gig.
Here are all the winners of the 2024 election, and what they’ve said.
Oakland mayor recall: Thao removed Mayor Sheng Thao was recalled with about 61% of the vote, a slightly smaller margin than the first results showed, but nowhere close enough to keep her seat. She appeared to concede on Nov. 8 in a statement touting her administration’s accomplishments on crime and homelessness.
“I am deeply proud of the progress we created together and I am committed to ensuring we stay on track by supporting a smooth transition,” she wrote . “It isn’t about me. It never has been. It has always been about Oakland.”
At-Large: Rowena Brown Labor-backed candidate and State Assembly staffer Rowena Brown is the winner of the at-large seat on the council. Longtime incumbent Rebecca Kaplan did not run for reelection, leaving a wide-open seat that several people tried to claim.
“We are ready for change and I am ready to lead,” Brown said on Nov. 15. “This win belongs to Oakland.” After ranked-choice tabulations, Brown is at 57% of the vote, and she received the most first-choice votes as well. Her closest competitor was former Police Chief LeRonne Armstrong.
District 1: Zac Unger Firefighter and union president Zac Unger swept the contest for the North Oakland council seat. He won by 82% after ranked-choice calculations, with accountant Len Raphael receiving 17%. Unger said he will step down from the fire department and his union leadership role when he takes office.
District 3: Carroll Fife Incumbent Carroll Fife, former director of a tenant advocacy organization, hung onto her job representing West Oakland and downtown. After ranked-choice tabulations, she ended up with 59% of the vote.
Number-two candidate Warren Logan, a public affairs professional who worked in policy for Mayor Libby Schaaf, conceded on Wednesday. In an email thanking supporters, he said, “I was honored to stand alongside you, and to advocate for the policy ideas we developed that address the root causes of our inequities and protect the communities, businesses, resources, and cultures that are the roots of this city we all love.”
District 5: Noel Gallo Noel Gallo won his fourth term representing Fruitvale, with 59% of the votes after ranked-choice calculations.
While Gallo has not publicly declared victory, his opponent with the second-highest number of votes, Erin Armstrong, a policy advisor for Supervisor Nate Miley, has conceded. “While we did not win this election, together we raised our voices and made it clear that District 5 deserves better,” she wrote on Instagram .
District 7: Ken Houston After Councilmember Treva Reid announced just days before the deadline that she wouldn’t seek reelection, the future of her seat became a bigger question. Community organizer Ken Houston decided to enter the race after she dropped out. He’s emerged as the victor, with 52% of the vote after ranked-choice tallies.
Oakland City Attorney: Ryan Richardson The next city attorney will be Ryan Richardson, who is currently chief assistant attorney to Barbara Parker.
On Nov. 11, Richardson emailed his supporters noting that he was up 10 points over his opponent, Brenda Harbin-Forte. While there were still some ballots to count, he said, “I am confident that when this process is completed, I will have the honor of serving as Oakland’s next City Attorney.” Richardson received 59% of the vote.
He told The Oaklandside he’s already planning a transition that will ensure stability and put Oakland on a footing to deal with the incoming Trump administration.
Alameda County Board of Supervisors District 5: Nikki Fortunato Bas Emeryville City Councilmember John Bauters enjoyed a lead in the supervisor race for over a week after the election while ballots were still being counted, but Oakland City Council President Nikki Fortunato Bas overtook him on Nov. 15 and eventually claimed victory.
“I will bring an unflagging commitment and engage the community to serve every resident by expanding affordable housing and effective solutions to homelessness, accessible healthcare, good jobs, and safe communities,” she said in a statement on Friday.
Bauters conceded the same day, saying he’d called Bas to congratulate her.
“I’m extremely proud of the positive campaign we ran,” he wrote in an email thanking his supporters.
